cuisine: Condiment / Western; widely adopted in Singapore
region_of_origin: United States (commercialised by Heinz, 1870s); ubiquitous in Singapore
food_subgroup: Tomato-based condiment; processed tomato concentrate with added sugar and salt; reference serving 1 tablespoon (17 g)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for tomato ketchup as a food product; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Heinz Classic Tomato Ketchup is 1 tablespoon (17 g), providing approximately 18 kcal, 4 g sugar, and 161 mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Ketchup, tomato — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 11935)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for tomato ketchup (FDC ID 11935) is the primary nutrient anchor. Values are consistent with the Heinz Singapore product label (H.J. Heinz Company Singapore, accessed 2026-04-13) and cross-checked against USDA Branded Food entries for Heinz ketchup. Minor rounding differences between label and USDA are within acceptable tolerance. Per-100g values used as reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 55 for tomato ketchup is consistent with published GI database values for tomato-based condiments with high added sugar. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification scale, GI ≤55 is classified as low, GI 56–69 as medium, and GI ≥70 as high. A GI of exactly 55 therefore falls in the low category. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (55 × 4.25) / 100 ≈ 2.3, rounded to 2.2 per tablespoon (17 g × 25 g carbohydrate/100 g = 4.25 g carbohydrate). At 1–2 tablespoons, GL is low; at 4–6 tablespoons, GL rises to 8.8–13.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium density (950 mg/100 g); 161 mg per tablespoon; cumulative risk with heavy use
hypertension.guidance: At 1–2 tablespoons (17–34 g), sodium contribution is moderate (~161–322 mg); however, heavy dipping-sauce use of 4–6 tablespoons adds 644–966 mg sodium per meal occasion, which is clinically significant for hypertensive patients and should be actively counselled against; potassium content (325 mg/100 g) partially offsets the sodium effect.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (24 g/100 g; 4 g per tablespoon); GI 55 (low); GL rises sharply with heavy use
type2_diabetes.guidance: At 1–2 tablespoons, glycemic load is low (~2.2–4.4) and is acceptable for most T2DM patients; at 4–6 tablespoons used as a dipping sauce, GL from ketchup alone reaches 8.8–13.2 and added sugar intake reaches 12–24 g, making heavy ketchup use a clinically underreported source of dietary sugar that should be flagged during dietary review.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0 g/100 g); lycopene content (~10 mg/100 g) from processed tomatoes may confer cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tomato ketchup contains no saturated fat and no cholesterol, and the lycopene from concentrated processed tomatoes (~8–12 mg/100 g) is more bioavailable than from fresh tomatoes due to cis-isomer formation during heat processing; at tablespoon-level use, ketchup does not adversely affect cardiovascular risk, though the high sodium content warrants attention in patients with concurrent hypertension.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lycopene from processed tomatoes; antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Processed tomato ketchup provides approximately 8–12 mg lycopene per 100 g in a highly bioavailable cis-isomer form; lycopene has documented antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that may be relevant to RA management, making moderate ketchup use acceptable in this population.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tomato ketchup (Heinz Classic) is a commercially produced, ultra-processed tomato-based condiment made from concentrated tomatoes, sugar, vinegar, salt, and spices. It is one of the most widely used condiments in Singapore, appearing as a dipping sauce for french fries, chicken nuggets, and toasted bread, and as a cooking base for Singapore-style fried rice and noodles. The texture is smooth and thick; the flavour is sweet-tangy with a mild tomato character. Per 100 g: 105 kcal, 1.2 g protein, 0.2 g fat, 25 g carbohydrate, 24 g sugar, 950 mg sodium. Per standard tablespoon (17 g): approximately 18 kcal, 4 g sugar, 161 mg sodium. The primary clinical concern is heavy use as a dipping sauce — 4 to 6 tablespoons per meal occasion adds 12–24 g sugar (equivalent to 3–6 teaspoons of table sugar) and 644–966 mg sodium, making it a clinically underreported source of both sugar and sodium in Singapore dietary assessments. GI is 55 (low per University of Sydney scale); GL per tablespoon is low (~2.2). A genuine nutritional benefit is the lycopene content (~8–12 mg/100 g) from concentrated processed tomatoes, which is more bioavailable than lycopene from fresh tomatoes due to heat-induced cis-isomer conversion. Suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free diets. For T2DM patients: 1–2 tablespoons is acceptable; heavy dipping use is not recommended.
meal_context: cooking sauce for Singapore-style fried rice

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are cross-validated between USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 11935) and the Heinz Singapore product label. GI is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database published values for tomato ketchup. Ontology anchors (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are defensible and directly matched. All mandatory multilingual names are present.
notes: saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Heinz product label — tomato ketchup contains no saturated fat by formulation. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — plant-derived product with no animal fat. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero — plant-derived product. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero — no fortification reported on Heinz Singapore label. trans_fat_g is 0.0 confirmed per Heinz Singapore product label. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in USDA SR Legacy entry for tomato ketchup (FDC ID 11935) or on the Heinz Singapore product label. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the primary source entry used. Lycopene content (~8–12 mg/100 g) is noted as a clinically relevant phytonutrient but is not a standard per_100g field in this template. GI of 55 is classified as low per the University of Sydney GI Database standard thresholds (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70); the gi_category is set to 'low' accordingly. GL per serving calculated as (55 × 4.25) / 100 = 2.34, rounded to 2.2 per the input record's verified value.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index of 55. Per the University of Sydney GI Database standard classification, GI ≤55 is low, GI 56–69 is medium, GI ≥70 is high. A GI of exactly 55 falls in the low category. Updated gi_source source_match_notes and notes field to document this classification rationale explicitly. Updated health_context type2_diabetes key_factor to reflect 'GI 55 (low)' rather than 'GI ~55'. Updated llm_training natural_language_description to state 'GI is 55 (low per University of Sydney scale)' for consistency.
